item.title: Don't know if I'm missing something here?
item.content:encoded:
This is gonna have to be a -1 from me

+My thoughts
-My thoughts

This article confuses me, the first time I read through it I was mildly intrigued by the emotional aspect, but on the second read I just got really hung up on the logic of it. The Foundation thinks this guy is enough of a threat to give him a full blown PoI designation (for good reason, the guy had connections to some weird stuff going down in Korea and his mother could literally make anomalies), and yet they think it's safe to just let the guy go? Even if they thought that keeping him at the site would be a waste of resources why on earth did they not amnestize him?? Even if he turned out to be a civilian he still just waltzed on out of a Foundation site back into the regular world with at least a vague idea of what the SCPF does. This is a major information security breach and it's not even brought up in the article.

It just seems super contrived for the addendum at the end to make sense, even the first time I read through this article I just kept thinking "why didn't they give him a non anomalous replica of the snowglobe and erase his memory?" and if that's too much work for them why not just erase him getting the snowglobe in the first place?

This is a pretty major plot hole IMO but it doesn't help that it's basically what the entire 'punchline' of the article is based on, the anomaly is just an anomalous object with about two sentences about it's actual properties in the whole article, and the characters don't have enough development for me to really care about any of them.

this is a hard -1 for me

TL;DR why did no one think to amnestize this guy with ties to possible anomalous activity who's mad at the Foundation and has knowledge about it?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This article made me very sad, thank you +1
pogchamp +100

item.title: Re: SCP-5380: -1
item.content:encoded: No, I believe he's talking about that snowglobe entry in the Log of Anomalous items.
I didn’t expect this to hit this high.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Very sweet. While I did see the setup coming, I can't help but enjoy it nonetheless. Nice work :) Keep the tearjerkers coming, looks like I wasn't the only sap who enjoyed *laughs*

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: harsh crit

item.title: Simple yet tragic
item.content:encoded:
I really like this! The story of poor Victor is rather heartbreaking.
+1 from here.

item.title: Re: UP
item.content:encoded:
Hello!

No, this was not intentional, and thank you for bringing this to my attention! I'll edit that!

item.title: UP
item.content:encoded:
At first, the name of site in the article is Site-23, but at the end it becomes 53. Is it intentional or negligent?

I'm a translator in CN, a part of my question is above.

item.title: Eh
item.content:encoded: To be honest, I this this belongs on the Log of Anomalous Items.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I read this a few weeks ago actually and I remember liking it; I've only recently decided to start being active here in this community; so why not slap it with a +1 since I did enjoy it!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
First SCP I've ever actually read from you. So this is the type of writer you are. A sweet one who understands people.

This mirrors the first draft you ever showed me (of an unrelated skip) but the improvement you've made in these past few months is #-_#ing inspiring, man.

Keep being the sweet heart that you are, dude.

+9001

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: …he somehow found out the address of a scp foundation and wasn't amnestied?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
SCP-5380 is kept in Site-23 within its own item locker. SCP-5380 is restricted to Personnel with at least Level 2 Clearance. No further containment procedures are necessary.

I assume the capitalization of "Personnel" is intentional?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Short but amply emotionally impactful. I think the length helped to prevent this from sliding into melodrama, and the ending wasn't too obtusely vengeful. I like this, and it deserves a spot on the site. +1

item.title: Re: SCP-5380: -1
item.content:encoded: You're probably thinking of 251 or 2028 but there effects are pretty different from this one. 251 creates scenes of violence in the snow globe. 2028 captures people's nightmares inside it, and it causes a reality restructuring event that replicates the nightmare in the real world if it's broken. Neither of them change the weather when there shaken. I think the mother-son relationship examined in 5380 helps make it unique.

item.title: no vote for now
item.content:encoded:
PoI-1341: My mom and I never got in much contact after she moved, and you're taking the one and only thing that connects us together away from us.

The dialogue in the interview is a little bit stiff and unnatural. Also, the sentence should say "away from me"
I really get the sense that PoI - 1342's mother poured her feelings for her son into that snow globe and at the end when a snow globe full of rain arrives I believe that it is an expression of the boy's sadness.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Really enjoyed this. I would be devestated if I were in Victor's position, ngl. Good job. +1
Minor complaint: In the interview, you have "PoI1341: Is there anything else?" You may want to fix that.

item.title: SCP-5380: -1
item.content:encoded:
I'm gonna' have to agree with Roget here in regards to the article's bluntness and lack of emotional depth. It's an extremely uninteresting piece to me, and I just can't get myself invested in the barely developed characters.

And Dr Gallium's comment reminded me of how I felt a twinge of familiarity with the effect. I could've sworn we had another SCP, a really early one maybe in S1, that was a snowglobe that caused the stuff inside to appear all around when shaken. But maybe I'm just mistaken.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This feels inauthentic to me personally. It’s far too blunt in its setup and goes through the motions of an “emotional” piece without actually being naturally evocative. The description is staid and boring, with the interviews being little more than naked attempts to bilk the sympathy of the reader.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You are an anomalous treasure chest which rapidly generates new treasures. +1

item.title: Oof
item.content:encoded: Saw this on IRC and didn't get a chance to give crit, but definitely a good one. +1
Very very cute, just like when I read it for crit. Love this article. +1!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The feels of a man denied his last memento of a deceased loved one.

I dunno if it was intentional or not but the researcher who talked with him doesn't come off to me as cold, I could see a twinge of sympathy she felt but it was the sympathy of a stranger and she had a job to do.

+1 and some tissues too

item.title: Snowglobe Post
item.content:encoded:
"I let you into my world."
"…And then, you became my World."

—Ashiwarya Patra

Valued Critters: PlaguePJP, OCuin, DianaBerry, Dr Moned, KaraKatt, HarryBlank

Music for your soul: Eternal Moment
(you know, i couldn't find anything cool to fit the title so i just picked one of my most recent and frequently listened songs.)
(you people should also listen to more of this artist, they make very relaxing and laidback music)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Wow, I feel like I've read this somewhere! +1
